regions: South: AL, GA, MS, SC, NC, AR, TN, TX
Midwest: IA, IL, IN, OH, MO, KS, NE

what_they_did.daily_work: Rented and farmed land owned by someone else. Unlike sharecroppers, tenant farmers typically owned their own tools, mules, and seed, and paid rent either as a fixed cash amount or a share of the crop (usually 1/4 to 1/3). They had more autonomy over what to plant and how to farm.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Varied by region. Cotton South: planting (March-April), cultivation (May-July), harvest (Sept-Nov). Midwest grain: planting (April-May), cultivation (June-July), harvest (Aug-Oct). Winter months spent repairing equipment and negotiating next year's lease.
tools_and_equipment: Team of mules or horses (owned by tenant)
Wagon for hauling crops to market
what_they_did.typical_hours: Dawn to dusk during growing season; shorter days in winter

economics.typical_arrangement: Cash tenants paid fixed rent ($2-$5 per acre annually in the 1920s South, $5-$10 per acre in the Midwest). Share tenants paid 1/4 to 1/3 of the crop. Cash tenants bore more risk but kept more profit in good years.
economics.annual_income_range: Southern tenant farmers: $300-$600 per year (1920s-1930s). Midwest tenant farmers on better land: $600-$1,200 per year. The 1930 Census of Agriculture reported average gross farm income for tenants at approximately $900 nationally.
economics.comparison: Tenant farmers generally earned more than sharecroppers but less than owner-operators. A Midwest tenant farmer in the 1920s might clear $800 in a good year — roughly equivalent to a factory worker's annual wage.
economics.debt_cycle: Less trapped than sharecroppers but still vulnerable. Crop failures, falling commodity prices (especially during the 1920s agricultural depression), and the inability to build equity meant many tenant farmers never accumulated enough to buy land.

hazards: Farm equipment injuries (caught in machinery, kicked by livestock)
Dust and respiratory problems, especially on the Plains
Isolation and limited access to medical care
working_conditions.living_conditions: Quality varied widely. Southern tenants often lived in the same kind of unpainted frame houses as sharecroppers. Midwest tenants might occupy more substantial farmhouses with 3-4 rooms. Housing was provided by the landlord and often poorly maintained.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Entire family contributed labor. Women managed poultry, dairy, and gardens in addition to fieldwork. Children worked from age 8-10 and school attendance was irregular, especially in the South.
working_conditions.autonomy: More than sharecroppers — tenant farmers chose their own crops (within lease constraints), sold their own harvest, and managed their own schedules. Cash tenants had the most independence.

push_factors: Dust Bowl devastation on the Plains (1930s)
Falling crop prices during the agricultural depression (1920s-1930s)
AAA crop reduction programs that benefited landowners but displaced tenants
Inability to accumulate capital for land purchase
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Large-scale mechanized agriculture, corporate farming operations, and the consolidation of small tenant plots into larger commercial farms. The number of tenant farmers declined by 50% between 1935 and 1959.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} gave up the lease on the farm in {county} around {year}, it probably wasn't one bad harvest that decided it — it was the accumulation of years where the land gave back less than it took.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Farm tenure status (owner, cash tenant, share tenant), acreage, crops, livestock, and value of farm products sold
records_generated.where_to_find: National Archives (NARA), state archives, FamilySearch microfilm
records_generated.research_tip: The distinction between 'cash tenant' and 'share tenant' in census records indicates different economic circumstances — cash tenants were generally better off

records_generated.record_type: County land records and lease agreements
records_generated.what_it_shows: Names of landlord and tenant, acreage, lease terms, duration of agreement
records_generated.where_to_find: County recorder of deeds offices, state archives
records_generated.research_tip: Recorded leases are less common than one might expect — many agreements were oral or on informal paper. When they do exist, they reveal economic relationships and migration patterns.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Loan applications, farm plans, family financial data for tenants seeking government assistance
records_generated.research_tip: FSA records from the 1930s-1940s provide unusually detailed family economic portraits including income, debts, household inventories, and photographs

census_clues.what_to_look_for: The 1900-1940 censuses distinguish between farm owners and tenants. Look for 'R' (rented) in the home ownership column combined with 'Farmer' as occupation. The agricultural schedule provides additional detail on tenure type.
census_clues.red_flags: A family listed as tenant farmers who move to a different county every 1-2 census cycles may indicate chronic inability to find favorable terms, or land being sold out from under them.

cultural_context: Tenant farming occupied a middle rung on the agricultural ladder — above sharecropping but below ownership. The 'agricultural ladder' theory held that a young man would start as a laborer, advance to sharecropper, then tenant, and finally owner. In practice, most tenant farmers never made it to ownership. By 1930, 42% of all American farms were operated by tenants, a figure that alarmed policymakers and contributed to New Deal farm programs.

counter_narratives.claim: Tenant farming was not exclusively a Southern or racial phenomenon
counter_narratives.context: Tenant farming was widespread in the Midwest and Plains states among white farmers. In Iowa, over 50% of farms were tenant-operated by 1935. These Midwest tenants operated in a different economic context than Southern tenants — they farmed better land, had access to credit, and were not subject to the racial caste system that constrained Black tenants.

counter_narratives.claim: Some tenant farmers preferred renting to owning
counter_narratives.context: A minority of tenant farmers, particularly successful cash tenants in the Midwest, deliberately chose to rent rather than buy. Renting allowed them to farm more acreage than they could afford to purchase and avoid the risk of mortgage debt. This was a rational economic calculation, not a sign of failure.

source_references.author: Grubbs, Donald H.
source_references.title: Cry from the Cotton: The Southern Tenant Farmers' Union and the New Deal
source_references.notes: Documents the organizing efforts of tenant farmers and the impact of New Deal agricultural policies on their displacement

source_references.author: Hurt, R. Douglas
source_references.title: American Agriculture: A Brief History
source_references.notes: Provides national context for tenant farming within the broader arc of American agricultural history

source_references.author: Kirby, Jack Temple
source_references.title: Rural Worlds Lost: The American South, 1920-1960
source_references.notes: Examines the collapse of the Southern tenant farming system and its human consequences
